scenario.description: Driver GPS update volume increases during dinner rush, saturating Redis CPU and causing order placement latency to spike via dispatch-service geo-queries.
scenario.long_description: During dinner rush, driver-location-service scales from 2 to 8 replicas, quadrupling GEOADD write volume to the shared Redis instance (from ~10,000 to ~40,000 commands/sec). Redis is CPU-limited to 100m via Kubernetes CFS quotas. At baseline the 10k ops/sec consume roughly 50% of the CPU budget, leaving headroom for dispatch-service GEOSEARCH reads. When 8 replicas push 40k ops/sec the Redis event loop is throttled by CFS to ~1 cycle/sec (vs 55 at baseline), causing command latency to spike from p95 ~2ms to p95 >1s. The growing sorted set (10k to 40k driver entries) compounds the effect since each GEOSEARCH must scan more members. dispatch-service GEOSEARCH queries block for >500ms, which propagates through the synchronous order-gateway call chain (restaurant-service, order-service, dispatch-service, payment-service), pushing end-to-end order placement p95 above 1 second. The root cause is a noisy-neighbor pattern: high-volume GPS writes from driver-location-service saturate the shared Redis CPU, starving latency-sensitive geo-query reads used by dispatch-service. The fix is to separate Redis instances for write-heavy GPS updates and read-heavy geo-queries, or to impose CFS-aware rate limiting on GPS update frequency.
